Name: Suspension Bridge
Description: Bridge # 7 on E-W Traverse (Suspension Bridge Campground )
All Notes:
The geogrid curtain above the bridge was completely torn down between fall 2021 and spring 2022. Bridge received less extensive, but similar damage to 2013. It's still physically possible to cross, but the rock damage has sheered 3 suspension cables nearest the steel A-frame at the north end, causing a list and slight drop in the decking relative to the landing platform. The 'retaining wall' supporting the trail as it continues west into the park is also severely compromised. Additionally the 2 cables which supported the geogrid curtain are starting to girdle the trees they're anchored to.

The cost/value fields for this seem to have gotten bumped around when the bridges were reclassified, but according to old files the initial cost of the bridge was in the ball park of $150000, not including the extensive repairs in 2013/2014., Stein Valley Trail; Additional notes: all foot bridges "re-categorized" in the New Assets tab due to need to list chronologically and ease of understanding field location(s).; Item description: Suspension Bridge; Specifications: As of 2017 the approach to the Suspension Bridge was improved by attaching treated 4'X4' posts with vinyl coated cable (3/16") handrail handrail bolted to stringer with galvanized 1/2"X8' lag bolts; Comments: SEVENTH (7) bridge on E-W Traverse (from Lytton to Tundra); due to large boulder crashing through original bridge, the bridge was repaired and re-installed in 2013/2014 a LARGE portion of the costs are associated with helicopter time and engineer involvement; located at Suspension Bridge Campground

All Notes:
Copilot updated Length, Number of Steps, Pedestrian Railing, Material, Structure Position, Stairs were put in 2020 by the Rangers and Student Rangers.

In May 2021, R. MacLeod added extra steps and rope rail.

These steps are an improvement from the steep bank that had no stairs a few years back, but the stairs are still steep. Rangers recommend building new stairs that cut into the bank and provide a less steep gradient for steps.
